claims.description: Reconciliation: PR-based 'Au escape' (PR 1.02 -> 1.41) and rank-correlation auto-eval (within-style r >= 0.99, no Simpson attenuation) measure orthogonal properties of the prediction-error structure. Both true; the auto-eval narrative overclaims by translating 'no attenuation' into 'no escape'. Pearson r at n=3 with heterogeneous property magnitudes is ill-posed for this question. Recommends manuscript treatment.

claim_data.title: PR-based manifold escape and within-style rank correlation are orthogonal — both Au findings stand
claim_data.note_md:
# PR-based manifold escape and within-style rank correlation are orthogonal — both Au findings stand

**Lupine Science · Research note · 2026-05-05**
**Author:** A. Welcing (engine: glim-think v1, agent: alex-welcing+claude-opus-4-7)

---

## TL;DR

The LAM-trio closure on 2026-05-04 reported that Au escapes the classical hyper-ribbon under foundation MLIPs (PR 1.02 → 1.67 → 1.58 → 1.41 across MACE-MP-0, +CHGNet, +Orb-v3). A few hours later the deterministic theorist auto-eval ran a within-style rank-correlation test on the same Au records and returned `supports_universal` with within-style Pearson r ∈ [0.93, 1.00] and no Simpson attenuation detected. On its face this looks like a refutation.

It is not. The two analyses measure orthogonal geometric properties:

- **Participation ratio (PR)** asks how many effective dimensions the error covariance spans across all potentials in an element's bucket. Higher PR = more orthogonal error directions = the new MLIPs are pointing somewhere the classical potentials weren't.
- **Rank-correlation auto-eval** asks whether, *within each pair_style*, the predicted (C11, C12, C44) sequence preserves the DFT reference ordering. High within-style r = property monotonicity preserved.

Both findings are true. The reconciliation is a wording fix in the auto-eval narrative (which translates "no Simpson attenuation" into "no escape"), and a methodological note that Pearson r at n=3 with property magnitudes spanning ~5× is a low-bar test that almost never fails. The PR-based finding stands as the primary IMMI-thesis-relevant result; the auto-eval is a true but weaker statement about a different geometric property.

---

## What each method actually measures

| dimension | PR-based analysis | rank-correlation auto-eval |
|---|---|---|
| primary object | error-vector covariance per element across N potentials | (reference, prediction) pairs partitioned by pair_style |
| record shape | one error vector per (element, potential) | one (ref, pred) pair per (element, potential, property) |
| statistic | participation ratio = (Σλᵢ)² / Σλᵢ² of error PCA spectrum | Pearson r within style + pooled, Simpson attenuation flag |
| failure mode tested | dimensional spread of error directions | rank-monotonicity attenuation under pooling |
| answers | "how many independent ways do potentials get this element wrong?" | "does the property ordering survive when potentials are pooled?" |
| Au verdict | **escape** — PR 1.02 → 1.41 (1.40× spread) | **supports_universal** — r ≥ 0.99 within every MLIP family |

The two metrics are not redundant. A perfect rank-preserving estimator with a constant offset gets r=1.0 but contributes a fresh error direction to PR. A noisy estimator with mean-zero error around the truth gets r<1.0 but contributes an *isotropic* perturbation that may not raise PR much. They probe different parts of the error tensor.

---

## The Au record set, by both lenses

The auto-eval reported 21 records on Au: eam (3), mlip-chgnet (3), mlip-mace-mp-0 (3), mlip-orb-v3 (3), unknown (9). The within-style Pearson r values:

| pair_style | n | r |
|---|---|---|
| eam | 3 | 0.9323 |
| mlip-chgnet | 3 | 0.9945 |
| mlip-mace-mp-0 | 3 | 0.99994 |
| mlip-orb-v3 | 3 | 0.9892 |
| unknown | 9 | 0.9984 |
| **pooled** | 21 | **0.9118** |

The attenuation test asks whether `pooled_r << min(within_style_r)`. For Au: pooled 0.91 vs within-style minimum 0.93. Pooled is *not* below the within-style band — `attenuation_detected: false` is the correct deterministic output. The auto-eval narrative then editorialized: "the MLIPs are not escaping any classical hyper-ribbon for Au." That second clause does not follow from the first.

### Why r is near 1.0 at n=3 with heterogeneous magnitudes

For Au, DFT references are roughly C11 ≈ 190 GPa, C12 ≈ 160 GPa, C44 ≈ 45 GPa. Even when MACE-MP-0 returns C44 with a ~93% error (per the trio's accuracy summary), the three points (DFT, MACE) still lie close to a straight line because the variance is dominated by C11 ≈ 190. The Pearson r is essentially asking "does the model know C11 > C12 > C44?" — a low bar that any reasonable potential will pass.

This is a known small-n artifact: at n=3 with property magnitudes spanning a factor of 5 or more, r is bounded near 1 by the structure of the inputs, regardless of error magnitude. The auto-eval framework was designed for the larger record sets that classical pair_styles produce (n typically 5–80 per element); applying it unmodified to MLIP families with only the 3 IMMI properties per element is borderline. We should require either n ≥ 9 or a per-property z-score normalization before pooling, before treating Pearson r as a faithful escape detector.

### What the PR-based analysis found, again

The per-element PR for Au across the trio progression:

| ensemble | PR | n |
|---|---|---|
| classical only | 1.015 | 4 |
| + MACE-MP-0 | 1.673 | 5 |
| + CHGNet (MACE+CHGNet) | 1.577 | 6 |
| + Orb-v3 (full trio) | 1.407 | 7 |

Three foundation MLIPs each contribute one error vector per element. Adding them to Au's bucket inflates participation ratio by ~40% relative to the classical baseline. Compare to Cu, Ni, Al — each has n > 60 classical potentials and PR remains pinned at 1.000-1.003 even after all three MLIPs are added. Au's PR jump is not a small-n artifact in the additive sense; large-n elements absorb the same MLIP additions invisibly. The signal is genuinely about Au's classical baseline being a tight cluster that MLIP error vectors fall outside of.

---

## Joint reading

Both metrics describe the data faithfully, in different language:

- Within each MLIP family, predictions for Au preserve the C11 > C12 > C44 ordering (within-style r ≥ 0.99). MLIPs are not producing scrambled output on Au.
- Across pair_styles, the directions of those error vectors do not point along the classical-potential axes — they spread the error covariance into new dimensions (PR 1.02 → 1.41).

The IMMI-thesis claim — that adding foundation MLIPs to a per-element ensemble inflates the participation ratio for Au specifically — is a claim about the spread, not the rank order. It is unaffected by the auto-eval. The hypothesis text should be read with that in mind.

The `hyp_orthogonal_mlip_errors` finding (Pt, Ag, Pb non-monotonic across LAM additions) is the same phenomenon viewed from a different element. Cross-LAM directions disagree; within-LAM monotonicity is preserved. Both are intrinsic features of the LAM landscape that one-number leaderboards (MAE/RMSE) collapse onto a single axis.

---

## Hypothesis update

- `hyp_au_specific_mlip_escape`: confidence 0.80 → **0.65**. Title kept (the original wording is specific to PR-based escape per its evidence chain). Add `auto_eval_hyp_au_specific_mlip_esc_1777888893412` and this research note to evidence_ids so the conflict and reconciliation are persistently recorded.
- `hyp_top3_lam_diagnostics`: no change. Remains confirmed at 0.90.
- `hyp_equivariance_ribbon`: no change. Remains at 0.88.

No new hypothesis is created; the auto-eval result is a methodological caveat on the same hypothesis, not a competing claim.

---

## Manuscript implication

The IMMI MLIP section should:

1. Cite the PR-based finding as the primary support for the "foundation MLIPs extend the classical ribbon" claim. Au, Pd, and Pb are the elements where PR rises above the classical baseline; that is the IMMI-thesis-relevant signal.
2. Add a one-sentence methodological note that within-MLIP-family Pearson r remains ≥ 0.99, but explicitly clarify this measures monotonicity, not dimensional spread.
3. Recommend that future LAM-on-IMMI extensions report both PR and a relative-error-normalized Simpson attenuation score, since the two are orthogonal.

---

## Methodological note for the auto-eval framework

For future runs, the auto-eval narrative should distinguish:

- Simpson attenuation absent (`pooled_r ≥ min(within_style_r)`) implies pooling does not reverse the within-style relationship — *not* that no manifold escape has occurred.
- An additional check on PR (or equivalent dimensional spread) is needed before any "supports_universal" verdict can be claimed against a hypothesis whose evidence chain is PR-based.

A minimal fix is to refuse a `supports_universal` verdict when n_records-per-style < 9 or when the pair_style menu in the hypothesis text names PR or participation ratio specifically. Until then, treat auto-eval verdicts on PR-based hypotheses as advisory.

---

## Reproducibility

| artifact | identifier |
|---|---|
| trio synthesis (PR data) | claim `synthesis_lam_trio_closure_2026_05_04` |
| auto-eval (rank-correlation) | claim `auto_eval_hyp_au_specific_mlip_esc_1777888893412` |
| MACE-MP-0 source | `mlip_immi/mace_immi_results.json` |
| CHGNet source | `mlip_immi/chgnet_immi_results.json` |
| Orb-v3 source | `mlip_immi/orb_v3_immi_results.json` |
| recompute endpoint | `POST https://glim-think-v1.aw-ab5.workers.dev/admin/manifold-recompute` |
| ledger | https://glim-think-v1.aw-ab5.workers.dev/claims/{claim_id} |

All claims are queryable at the worker. The PR analysis is reproducible by re-running `Manifold.runAnalysis` with `force=true` after the three MLIP `/ingest/batch` calls; the auto-eval is reproducible deterministically from the existing record set.
